site.title: Interactive Encyclopedia of Color
site.tagline: A museum-grade laboratory of color science.
sine.title: Anatomy of a Sine Wave
sine.subtitle: drag three sliders — amplitude, wavelength, phase — and watch each parameter reshape the same curve
sine.definition: Every periodic wave can be described by three numbers: how tall, how long, and where its cycle begins.
sine.guide: Slide the amplitude to make the wave taller. Slide the wavelength to stretch one cycle wider. Slide the phase to shift the whole curve left or right.
snell.title: Snell's Law Refractometer
snell.subtitle: drag the incident ray and watch n₁ sin θ₁ = n₂ sin θ₂ rebalance in real time
snell.definition: Light bends at an interface because its phase velocity changes between media of different refractive index.
snell.guide: Drag the incident angle slider to tilt the laser. Slide the lower-medium index to see the refracted ray bend toward or away from the normal. Past the critical angle, only total internal reflection remains.
soap.title: Soap Bubble Thin-Film Interference
soap.subtitle: slide the film thickness from 50 to 1000 nanometres and watch the reflected colour cycle through the structural rainbow
soap.definition: Light reflected from the top and bottom of a thin film interferes — constructively for some wavelengths, destructively for others, painting the bubble in structural colour.
soap.guide: Slide the thickness to sweep the film from too-thin to too-thick. Slide the refractive index to switch between soap, oil, and silicate films. The displayed colour is the spectrally-integrated reflectance.
sky.title: Why the Sky Is Blue
sky.subtitle: move the sun across the sky and watch Rayleigh scattering paint the daytime blue and the sunset red
sky.definition: Air molecules scatter shorter wavelengths far more strongly than longer ones — by a factor of 1/λ⁴ — leaving blue everywhere we don't look at the sun and red along the path that does.
sky.guide: Drag the sun across the sky. The colour of the zenith and the colour of the sun's direction update from the spectral integral of Rayleigh scattering over the air column.
cvd.title: Everyday Color Confusion
cvd.subtitle: toggle between trichromat and dichromat views to see which colour pairs collapse into the same percept
cvd.definition: About one in twelve men and one in two hundred women see colour differently — most often confusing red with green, sometimes blue with yellow.
cvd.guide: Click the four buttons to switch the page through normal, protanope, deuteranope, and tritanope vision. Notice which pairs of colours become indistinguishable.
